[[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City), [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City, Kaneyama Town) | Range = Kamuro Range | Type = Fault-block mountain ([[Granite]]) | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|38.902230|140.492306}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Kamuro-yama }} '''Kamuro-yama''' (神室山) is a mountain that straddles the borders of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City) and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City and Kaneyama Town), serving as the main peak of the Kamuro Range. It is one of the '''{{ill|200 Famous Japanese Mountains|ja|日本二百名山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/200 Famous Japanese Mountains|qq=}}'''. This article also describes the Kamuro Range (Kamuro Mountain Range) in general. == Overview == The Kamuro Range, with Kamuro-yama as its main peak, stretches for over 30 km, comprising peaks such as {{ill|Kuro-mori|ja|黒森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuro-mori|qq=}} (1,057 m), {{ill|Suishō-mori|ja|水晶森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suishō-mori|qq=}} (1,097 m), {{ill|Mae-Kamuro-yama|ja|前神室山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-Kamuro-yama|qq=}} (1,342 m), Kamuro-yama (1,365 m), {{ill|Tengu-mori|ja|天狗森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu-mori|qq=}} (1,302 m), {{ill|Komata-yama|ja|小又山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komata-yama|qq=}} (1,366 m), {{ill|Hiuchi-dake|ja|火打岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiuchi-dake|qq=}} (1,237 m), {{ill|Hachihori-yama|ja|八森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachihori-yama|qq=}} (1,098 m), {{ill|Mokuzō-yama|ja|杢蔵山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuzō-yama|qq=}} (1,026 m), and {{ill|Ōmori-yama|ja|大森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori-yama|qq=}} (591 m). Although not exceptionally tall, its rugged appearance, sculpted by heavy snowfall, has earned it the nickname "The Mini-Alps of Tōhoku." The entire range is part of the [[:en:Kurikoma Quasi-National Park|Kurikoma Quasi-National Park]]. Kamuro-yama has been a site of [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] since ancient times, comparable to [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]] as a sacred mountain for ascetic practices. According to legend, it was opened by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] during his eastern expeditions. The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. [[File:Kamurosan Santyô.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Summit of Kamuro-yama (August 2011)]] == Access == === Akita Prefecture (Yuzawa City) === * '''Yakunai Trailhead''' ** '''Nishinokuchi Route''': This route follows Nishinokuchi River to the summit, featuring stone statues of Fudō Myōō, steep climbs like "Munazuka Hatchō," and scenic wetlands at Onta-no-Kami. ** '''Panorama Route''': This route passes Mae-Kamuro-yama and joins the Araya Route, featuring Kabutayama Shrine and scenic viewpoints. === Yamagata Prefecture (Kaneyama Town) === * '''Suishō-mori Trailhead''': Once a major route through the [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]], this trail leads to Suishō-mori and Mae-Kamuro-yama. * '''Araya Trailhead''': A route along Ōtaki Gorge leading to the summit. === Yamagata Prefecture (Shinjō City) === * '''Donai Trailhead''': This route joins the Kamazawa Trail and ascends via the Daiyama Ridge. * '''Sariguchi Trailhead''': A path through Donai Gorge to Sariguchi. * '''Hiuchi Shinmichi Trailhead''': Directly ascends Hiuchi-dake. * '''Hagino Trailhead''': A route to Hachihori-yama via Ippaimori. * '''San’ya Trailhead''': Passes through Mokuzō-yama and connects to other ridge trails. === Yamagata Prefecture (Mogami Town) === * '''Yakushihara Trailhead''': Ascends to Hachihori-yama. * '''Oyagurumi Trailhead''': Leads to the ridge between Komata-yama and Hachihori-yama. * '''Nishinokuchi Trailhead''': Starts near Shirakawa Dam and ascends Komata-yama. == Gallery == 三十三尋の滝２.jpg|Sanju-sanjihiro Falls on the Nishinokuchi Route 胸突八丁坂２.jpg|Munazuka Hatchō slope on the Nishinokuchi Route クマのひるね坂から御田の神.jpg|Onta-no-Kami seen from Kuma no Hirune slope 前神室山と雄勝・秋ノ宮の集落２.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama with Ogatsu and Akinomiya settlements 神室山山頂２.jpg|Summit of Kamuro-yama 避難小屋と雲海の広がる新庄盆地２.jpg|Shelter and sea of clouds over the Shinjō Basin (Gassan in the background) 栗駒山と虎毛山２.jpg|[[:en:Mount Kurikoma|Mount Kurikoma]] (left) and {{ill|Torage-yama|ja|虎毛山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torage-yama|qq=}} (right) 前神室山.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama == Supplementary Notes == * Nearby is another mountain called {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:1000-meter mountains]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[en:Kamuro-yama]]

According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:1000-meter mountains]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[en:Kamuro-yama]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:山岳名目録
revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | Name = Kamuro-yama (神室山) | image = [[File:Kamuroyama.jpg|300px]] | Caption = Kamuro-yama and an emergency shelter (August 2011) | Elevation = 1365.2m | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|54|8|N|140|29|32.3|E|type:mountain_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{flag|Japan}}
[[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City), [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City, Kaneyama Town) | Range = Kamuro Range | Type = Fault-block mountain ([[Granite]]) | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|38.902230|140.492306}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Kamuro-yama }} '''Kamuro-yama''' (神室山) is a mountain that straddles the borders of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City) and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City and Kaneyama Town), serving as the main peak of the Kamuro Range. It is one of the '''{{ill|200 Famous Japanese Mountains|ja|日本二百名山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/200 Famous Japanese Mountains|qq=}}'''. This article also describes the Kamuro Range (Kamuro Mountain Range) in general. == Overview == The Kamuro Range, with Kamuro-yama as its main peak, stretches for over 30 km, comprising peaks such as {{ill|Kuro-mori|ja|黒森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuro-mori|qq=}} (1,057 m), {{ill|Suishō-mori|ja|水晶森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suishō-mori|qq=}} (1,097 m), {{ill|Mae-Kamuro-yama|ja|前神室山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-Kamuro-yama|qq=}} (1,342 m), Kamuro-yama (1,365 m), {{ill|Tengu-mori|ja|天狗森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu-mori|qq=}} (1,302 m), {{ill|Komata-yama|ja|小又山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komata-yama|qq=}} (1,366 m), {{ill|Hiuchi-dake|ja|火打岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiuchi-dake|qq=}} (1,237 m), {{ill|Hachihori-yama|ja|八森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachihori-yama|qq=}} (1,098 m), {{ill|Mokuzō-yama|ja|杢蔵山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuzō-yama|qq=}} (1,026 m), and {{ill|Ōmori-yama|ja|大森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori-yama|qq=}} (591 m). Although not exceptionally tall, its rugged appearance, sculpted by heavy snowfall, has earned it the nickname "The Mini-Alps of Tōhoku." The entire range is part of the [[:en:Kurikoma Quasi-National Park|Kurikoma Quasi-National Park]]. Kamuro-yama has been a site of [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] since ancient times, comparable to [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]] as a sacred mountain for ascetic practices. According to legend, it was opened by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] during his eastern expeditions. The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. [[File:Kamurosan Santyô.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Summit of Kamuro-yama (August 2011)]] == Access == === Akita Prefecture (Yuzawa City) === * '''Yakunai Trailhead''' ** '''Nishinokuchi Route''': This route follows Nishinokuchi River to the summit, featuring stone statues of Fudō Myōō, steep climbs like "Munazuka Hatchō," and scenic wetlands at Onta-no-Kami. ** '''Panorama Route''': This route passes Mae-Kamuro-yama and joins the Araya Route, featuring Kabutayama Shrine and scenic viewpoints. === Yamagata Prefecture (Kaneyama Town) === * '''Suishō-mori Trailhead''': Once a major route through the [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]], this trail leads to Suishō-mori and Mae-Kamuro-yama. * '''Araya Trailhead''': A route along Ōtaki Gorge leading to the summit. === Yamagata Prefecture (Shinjō City) === * '''Donai Trailhead''': This route joins the Kamazawa Trail and ascends via the Daiyama Ridge. * '''Sariguchi Trailhead''': A path through Donai Gorge to Sariguchi. * '''Hiuchi Shinmichi Trailhead''': Directly ascends Hiuchi-dake. * '''Hagino Trailhead''': A route to Hachihori-yama via Ippaimori. * '''San’ya Trailhead''': Passes through Mokuzō-yama and connects to other ridge trails. === Yamagata Prefecture (Mogami Town) === * '''Yakushihara Trailhead''': Ascends to Hachihori-yama. * '''Oyagurumi Trailhead''': Leads to the ridge between Komata-yama and Hachihori-yama. * '''Nishinokuchi Trailhead''': Starts near Shirakawa Dam and ascends Komata-yama. == Gallery == 三十三尋の滝２.jpg|Sanju-sanjihiro Falls on the Nishinokuchi Route 胸突八丁坂２.jpg|Munazuka Hatchō slope on the Nishinokuchi Route クマのひるね坂から御田の神.jpg|Onta-no-Kami seen from Kuma no Hirune slope 前神室山と雄勝・秋ノ宮の集落２.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama with Ogatsu and Akinomiya settlements 神室山山頂２.jpg|Summit of Kamuro-yama 避難小屋と雲海の広がる新庄盆地２.jpg|Shelter and sea of clouds over the Shinjō Basin (Gassan in the background) 栗駒山と虎毛山２.jpg|[[:en:Mount Kurikoma|Mount Kurikoma]] (left) and {{ill|Torage-yama|ja|虎毛山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torage-yama|qq=}} (right) 前神室山.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama == Supplementary Notes == * Nearby is another mountain called {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:1000-meter mountains]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[en:Kamuro-yama]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]]

The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. [[File:Kamurosan Santyô.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Summit of Kamuro-yama (August 2011)]] == Access == === Akita Prefecture (Yuzawa City) === * '''Yakunai Trailhead''' ** '''Nishinokuchi Route''': This route follows Nishinokuchi River to the summit, featuring stone statues of Fudō Myōō, steep climbs like "Munazuka Hatchō," and scenic wetlands at Onta-no-Kami. ** '''Panorama Route''': This route passes Mae-Kamuro-yama and joins the Araya Route, featuring Kabutayama Shrine and scenic viewpoints. === Yamagata Prefecture (Kaneyama Town) === * '''Suishō-mori Trailhead''': Once a major route through the [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]], this trail leads to Suishō-mori and Mae-Kamuro-yama. * '''Araya Trailhead''': A route along Ōtaki Gorge leading to the summit. === Yamagata Prefecture (Shinjō City) === * '''Donai Trailhead''': This route joins the Kamazawa Trail and ascends via the Daiyama Ridge. * '''Sariguchi Trailhead''': A path through Donai Gorge to Sariguchi. * '''Hiuchi Shinmichi Trailhead''': Directly ascends Hiuchi-dake. * '''Hagino Trailhead''': A route to Hachihori-yama via Ippaimori. * '''San’ya Trailhead''': Passes through Mokuzō-yama and connects to other ridge trails. === Yamagata Prefecture (Mogami Town) === * '''Yakushihara Trailhead''': Ascends to Hachihori-yama. * '''Oyagurumi Trailhead''': Leads to the ridge between Komata-yama and Hachihori-yama. * '''Nishinokuchi Trailhead''': Starts near Shirakawa Dam and ascends Komata-yama. == Gallery == 三十三尋の滝２.jpg|Sanju-sanjihiro Falls on the Nishinokuchi Route 胸突八丁坂２.jpg|Munazuka Hatchō slope on the Nishinokuchi Route クマのひるね坂から御田の神.jpg|Onta-no-Kami seen from Kuma no Hirune slope 前神室山と雄勝・秋ノ宮の集落２.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama with Ogatsu and Akinomiya settlements 神室山山頂２.jpg|Summit of Kamuro-yama 避難小屋と雲海の広がる新庄盆地２.jpg|Shelter and sea of clouds over the Shinjō Basin (Gassan in the background) 栗駒山と虎毛山２.jpg|[[:en:Mount Kurikoma|Mount Kurikoma]] (left) and {{ill|Torage-yama|ja|虎毛山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torage-yama|qq=}} (right) 前神室山.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama == Supplementary Notes == * Nearby is another mountain called {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:1000-meter mountains]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[en:Kamuro-yama]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]]

{{Infobox mountain | Name = Kamuro-yama (神室山) | image = [[File:Kamuroyama.jpg|300px]] | Caption = Kamuro-yama and an emergency shelter (August 2011) | Elevation = 1365.2m | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|54|8|N|140|29|32.3|E|type:mountain_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{flag|Japan}}
[[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City), [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City, Kaneyama Town) | Range = Kamuro Range | Type = Fault-block mountain ([[Granite]]) | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|38.902230|140.492306}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Kamuro-yama }} '''Kamuro-yama''' (神室山) is a mountain that straddles the borders of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City) and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City and Kaneyama Town), serving as the main peak of the Kamuro Range. It is one of the '''{{ill|200 Famous Japanese Mountains|ja|日本二百名山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/200 Famous Japanese Mountains|qq=}}'''. This article also describes the Kamuro Range (Kamuro Mountain Range) in general. == Overview == The Kamuro Range, with Kamuro-yama as its main peak, stretches for over 30 km, comprising peaks such as {{ill|Kuro-mori|ja|黒森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuro-mori|qq=}} (1,057 m), {{ill|Suishō-mori|ja|水晶森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suishō-mori|qq=}} (1,097 m), {{ill|Mae-Kamuro-yama|ja|前神室山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-Kamuro-yama|qq=}} (1,342 m), Kamuro-yama (1,365 m), {{ill|Tengu-mori|ja|天狗森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu-mori|qq=}} (1,302 m), {{ill|Komata-yama|ja|小又山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komata-yama|qq=}} (1,366 m), {{ill|Hiuchi-dake|ja|火打岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiuchi-dake|qq=}} (1,237 m), {{ill|Hachihori-yama|ja|八森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachihori-yama|qq=}} (1,098 m), {{ill|Mokuzō-yama|ja|杢蔵山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuzō-yama|qq=}} (1,026 m), and {{ill|Ōmori-yama|ja|大森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori-yama|qq=}} (591 m). Although not exceptionally tall, its rugged appearance, sculpted by heavy snowfall, has earned it the nickname "The Mini-Alps of Tōhoku." The entire range is part of the [[:en:Kurikoma Quasi-National Park|Kurikoma Quasi-National Park]]. Kamuro-yama has been a site of [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] since ancient times, comparable to [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]] as a sacred mountain for ascetic practices. According to legend, it was opened by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] during his eastern expeditions. The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:1000-meter mountains]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[en:Kamuro-yama]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:湯沢市の地理]]

According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:1000-meter mountains]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[en:Kamuro-yama]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:湯沢市の地理]] [[Category:秋田県の自然景勝地]]

revision.comment: Bot: update category → Geography of Yuzawa, Akita
revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | Name = Kamuro-yama (神室山) | image = [[File:Kamuroyama.jpg|300px]] | Caption = Kamuro-yama and an emergency shelter (August 2011) | Elevation = 1365.2m | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|54|8|N|140|29|32.3|E|type:mountain_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{flag|Japan}}
[[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City), [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City, Kaneyama Town) | Range = Kamuro Range | Type = Fault-block mountain ([[Granite]]) | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|38.902230|140.492306}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Kamuro-yama }} '''Kamuro-yama''' (神室山) is a mountain that straddles the borders of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City) and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City and Kaneyama Town), serving as the main peak of the Kamuro Range. It is one of the '''{{ill|200 Famous Japanese Mountains|ja|日本二百名山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/200 Famous Japanese Mountains|qq=}}'''. This article also describes the Kamuro Range (Kamuro Mountain Range) in general. == Overview == The Kamuro Range, with Kamuro-yama as its main peak, stretches for over 30 km, comprising peaks such as {{ill|Kuro-mori|ja|黒森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuro-mori|qq=}} (1,057 m), {{ill|Suishō-mori|ja|水晶森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suishō-mori|qq=}} (1,097 m), {{ill|Mae-Kamuro-yama|ja|前神室山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-Kamuro-yama|qq=}} (1,342 m), Kamuro-yama (1,365 m), {{ill|Tengu-mori|ja|天狗森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu-mori|qq=}} (1,302 m), {{ill|Komata-yama|ja|小又山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komata-yama|qq=}} (1,366 m), {{ill|Hiuchi-dake|ja|火打岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiuchi-dake|qq=}} (1,237 m), {{ill|Hachihori-yama|ja|八森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachihori-yama|qq=}} (1,098 m), {{ill|Mokuzō-yama|ja|杢蔵山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuzō-yama|qq=}} (1,026 m), and {{ill|Ōmori-yama|ja|大森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori-yama|qq=}} (591 m). Although not exceptionally tall, its rugged appearance, sculpted by heavy snowfall, has earned it the nickname "The Mini-Alps of Tōhoku." The entire range is part of the [[:en:Kurikoma Quasi-National Park|Kurikoma Quasi-National Park]]. Kamuro-yama has been a site of [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] since ancient times, comparable to [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]] as a sacred mountain for ascetic practices. According to legend, it was opened by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] during his eastern expeditions. The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. [[File:Kamurosan Santyô.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Summit of Kamuro-yama (August 2011)]] == Access == === Akita Prefecture (Yuzawa City) === * '''Yakunai Trailhead''' ** '''Nishinokuchi Route''': This route follows Nishinokuchi River to the summit, featuring stone statues of Fudō Myōō, steep climbs like "Munazuka Hatchō," and scenic wetlands at Onta-no-Kami. ** '''Panorama Route''': This route passes Mae-Kamuro-yama and joins the Araya Route, featuring Kabutayama Shrine and scenic viewpoints. === Yamagata Prefecture (Kaneyama Town) === * '''Suishō-mori Trailhead''': Once a major route through the [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]], this trail leads to Suishō-mori and Mae-Kamuro-yama. * '''Araya Trailhead''': A route along Ōtaki Gorge leading to the summit. === Yamagata Prefecture (Shinjō City) === * '''Donai Trailhead''': This route joins the Kamazawa Trail and ascends via the Daiyama Ridge. * '''Sariguchi Trailhead''': A path through Donai Gorge to Sariguchi. * '''Hiuchi Shinmichi Trailhead''': Directly ascends Hiuchi-dake. * '''Hagino Trailhead''': A route to Hachihori-yama via Ippaimori. * '''San’ya Trailhead''': Passes through Mokuzō-yama and connects to other ridge trails. === Yamagata Prefecture (Mogami Town) === * '''Yakushihara Trailhead''': Ascends to Hachihori-yama. * '''Oyagurumi Trailhead''': Leads to the ridge between Komata-yama and Hachihori-yama. * '''Nishinokuchi Trailhead''': Starts near Shirakawa Dam and ascends Komata-yama. == Gallery == 三十三尋の滝２.jpg|Sanju-sanjihiro Falls on the Nishinokuchi Route 胸突八丁坂２.jpg|Munazuka Hatchō slope on the Nishinokuchi Route クマのひるね坂から御田の神.jpg|Onta-no-Kami seen from Kuma no Hirune slope 前神室山と雄勝・秋ノ宮の集落２.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama with Ogatsu and Akinomiya settlements 神室山山頂２.jpg|Summit of Kamuro-yama 避難小屋と雲海の広がる新庄盆地２.jpg|Shelter and sea of clouds over the Shinjō Basin (Gassan in the background) 栗駒山と虎毛山２.jpg|[[:en:Mount Kurikoma|Mount Kurikoma]] (left) and {{ill|Torage-yama|ja|虎毛山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torage-yama|qq=}} (right) 前神室山.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama == Supplementary Notes == * Nearby is another mountain called {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:1000-meter mountains]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[en:Kamuro-yama]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa, Akita]] [[Category:秋田県の自然景勝地]]

It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. [[File:Kamurosan Santyô.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Summit of Kamuro-yama (August 2011)]] == Access == === Akita Prefecture (Yuzawa City) === * '''Yakunai Trailhead''' ** '''Nishinokuchi Route''': This route follows Nishinokuchi River to the summit, featuring stone statues of Fudō Myōō, steep climbs like "Munazuka Hatchō," and scenic wetlands at Onta-no-Kami. ** '''Panorama Route''': This route passes Mae-Kamuro-yama and joins the Araya Route, featuring Kabutayama Shrine and scenic viewpoints. === Yamagata Prefecture (Kaneyama Town) === * '''Suishō-mori Trailhead''': Once a major route through the [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]], this trail leads to Suishō-mori and Mae-Kamuro-yama. * '''Araya Trailhead''': A route along Ōtaki Gorge leading to the summit. === Yamagata Prefecture (Shinjō City) === * '''Donai Trailhead''': This route joins the Kamazawa Trail and ascends via the Daiyama Ridge. * '''Sariguchi Trailhead''': A path through Donai Gorge to Sariguchi. * '''Hiuchi Shinmichi Trailhead''': Directly ascends Hiuchi-dake. * '''Hagino Trailhead''': A route to Hachihori-yama via Ippaimori. * '''San’ya Trailhead''': Passes through Mokuzō-yama and connects to other ridge trails. === Yamagata Prefecture (Mogami Town) === * '''Yakushihara Trailhead''': Ascends to Hachihori-yama. * '''Oyagurumi Trailhead''': Leads to the ridge between Komata-yama and Hachihori-yama. * '''Nishinokuchi Trailhead''': Starts near Shirakawa Dam and ascends Komata-yama. == Gallery == 三十三尋の滝２.jpg|Sanju-sanjihiro Falls on the Nishinokuchi Route 胸突八丁坂２.jpg|Munazuka Hatchō {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:1000-meter mountains]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa, Akita]] [[Category:秋田県の自然景勝地]] [[ceb:Kamuro Yama]] {{Commons category|Mount Kamuro (Akita and Yamagata)}}

{{Infobox mountain | Name = Kamuro-yama (神室山) | image = [[File:Kamuroyama.jpg|300px]] | Caption = Kamuro-yama and an emergency shelter (August 2011) | Elevation = 1365.2m | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|54|8|N|140|29|32.3|E|type:mountain_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{flag|Japan}}
[[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City), [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City, Kaneyama Town) | Range = Kamuro Range | Type = Fault-block mountain ([[Granite]]) | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|38.902230|140.492306}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Kamuro-yama }} '''Kamuro-yama''' (神室山) is a mountain that straddles the borders of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City) and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City and Kaneyama Town), serving as the main peak of the Kamuro Range. It is one of the '''{{ill|200 Famous Japanese Mountains|ja|日本二百名山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/200 Famous Japanese Mountains|qq=}}'''. This article also describes the Kamuro Range (Kamuro Mountain Range) in general. == Overview == The Kamuro Range, with Kamuro-yama as its main peak, stretches for over 30 km, comprising peaks such as {{ill|Kuro-mori|ja|黒森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuro-mori|qq=}} (1,057 m), {{ill|Suishō-mori|ja|水晶森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suishō-mori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,097 m), {{ill|Mae-Kamuro-yama|ja|前神室山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-Kamuro-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,342 m), Kamuro-yama (1,365 m), {{ill|Tengu-mori|ja|天狗森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu-mori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,302 m), {{ill|Komata-yama|ja|小又山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komata-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,366 m), {{ill|Hiuchi-dake|ja|火打岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiuchi-dake|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,237 m), {{ill|Hachihori-yama|ja|八森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachihori-yama|qq=}} (1,098 m), {{ill|Mokuzō-yama|ja|杢蔵山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuzō-yama|qq=}} (1,026 m), and {{ill|Ōmori-yama|ja|大森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori-yama|qq=}} (591 m). Although not exceptionally tall, its rugged appearance, sculpted by heavy snowfall, has earned it the nickname "The Mini-Alps of Tōhoku." The entire range is part of the [[:en:Kurikoma Quasi-National Park|Kurikoma Quasi-National Park]]. Kamuro-yama has been a site of [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] since ancient times, comparable to [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]] as a sacred mountain for ascetic practices. According to legend, it was opened by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] during his eastern expeditions. The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa, Akita]] [[Category:秋田県の自然景勝地]] [[ceb:Kamuro Yama]] {{Commons category|Mount Kamuro (Akita and Yamagata)}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Chokaisan Faith
revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | Name = Kamuro-yama (神室山) | image = [[File:Kamuroyama.jpg|300px]] | Caption = Kamuro-yama and an emergency shelter (August 2011) | Elevation = 1365.2m | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|54|8|N|140|29|32.3|E|type:mountain_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{flag|Japan}}
[[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City), [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City, Kaneyama Town) | Range = Kamuro Range | Type = Fault-block mountain ([[Granite]]) | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|38.902230|140.492306}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Kamuro-yama }} '''Kamuro-yama''' (神室山) is a mountain that straddles the borders of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City) and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City and Kaneyama Town), serving as the main peak of the Kamuro Range. It is one of the '''{{ill|200 Famous Japanese Mountains|ja|日本二百名山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/200 Famous Japanese Mountains|qq=}}'''. This article also describes the Kamuro Range (Kamuro Mountain Range) in general. == Overview == The Kamuro Range, with Kamuro-yama as its main peak, stretches for over 30 km, comprising peaks such as {{ill|Kuro-mori|ja|黒森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuro-mori|qq=}} (1,057 m), {{ill|Suishō-mori|ja|水晶森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suishō-mori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,097 m), {{ill|Mae-Kamuro-yama|ja|前神室山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-Kamuro-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,342 m), Kamuro-yama (1,365 m), {{ill|Tengu-mori|ja|天狗森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu-mori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,302 m), {{ill|Komata-yama|ja|小又山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komata-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,366 m), {{ill|Hiuchi-dake|ja|火打岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiuchi-dake|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,237 m), {{ill|Hachihori-yama|ja|八森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachihori-yama|qq=}} (1,098 m), {{ill|Mokuzō-yama|ja|杢蔵山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuzō-yama|qq=}} (1,026 m), and {{ill|Ōmori-yama|ja|大森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori-yama|qq=}} (591 m). Although not exceptionally tall, its rugged appearance, sculpted by heavy snowfall, has earned it the nickname "The Mini-Alps of Tōhoku." The entire range is part of the [[:en:Kurikoma Quasi-National Park|Kurikoma Quasi-National Park]]. Kamuro-yama has been a site of [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] since ancient times, comparable to [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]] as a sacred mountain for ascetic practices. According to legend, it was opened by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] during his eastern expeditions. The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. [[File:Kamurosan Santyô.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Summit of Kamuro-yama (August 2011)]] == Access == === Akita Prefecture (Yuzawa City) === * '''Yakunai Trailhead''' ** '''Nishinokuchi Route''': This route follows Nishinokuchi River to the summit, featuring stone statues of Fudō Myōō, steep climbs like "Munazuka Hatchō," and scenic wetlands at Onta-no-Kami. ** '''Panorama Route''': This route passes Mae-Kamuro-yama and joins the Araya Route, featuring Kabutayama Shrine and scenic viewpoints. === Yamagata Prefecture (Kaneyama Town) === * '''Suishō-mori Trailhead''': Once a major route through the [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]], this trail leads to Suishō-mori and Mae-Kamuro-yama. * '''Araya Trailhead''': A route along Ōtaki Gorge leading to the summit. === Yamagata Prefecture (Shinjō City) === * '''Donai Trailhead''': This route joins the Kamazawa Trail and ascends via the Daiyama Ridge. * '''Sariguchi Trailhead''': A path through Donai Gorge to Sariguchi. * '''Hiuchi Shinmichi Trailhead''': Directly ascends Hiuchi-dake. * '''Hagino Trailhead''': A route to Hachihori-yama via Ippaimori. * '''San’ya Trailhead''': Passes through Mokuzō-yama and connects to other ridge trails. === Yamagata Prefecture (Mogami Town) === * '''Yakushihara Trailhead''': Ascends to Hachihori-yama. * '''Oyagurumi Trailhead''': Leads to the ridge between Komata-yama and Hachihori-yama. * '''Nishinokuchi Trailhead''': Starts near Shirakawa Dam and ascends Komata-yama. == Gallery == 三十三尋の滝２.jpg|Sanju-sanjihiro Falls on the Nishinokuchi Route 胸突八丁坂２.jpg|Munazuka Hatchō slope on the Nishinokuchi Route クマのひるね坂から御田の神.jpg|Onta-no-Kami seen from Kuma no Hirune slope 前神室山と雄勝・秋ノ宮の集落２.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama with Ogatsu and Akinomiya settlements 神室山山頂２.jpg|Summit of Kamuro-yama 避難小屋と雲海の広がる新庄盆地２.jpg|Shelter and sea of clouds over the Shinjō Basin (Gassan in the background) 栗駒山と虎毛山２.jpg|[[:en:Mount Kurikoma|Mount Kurikoma]] (left) and {{ill|Torage-yama|ja|虎毛山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torage-yama|qq=}} (right) 前神室山.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama == Supplementary Notes == * Nearby is another mountain called {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountain ranges in Japan]] [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa City]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa, Akita]] [[Category:秋田県の自然景勝地]] [[ceb:Kamuro Yama]] {{Commons category|Mount Kamuro (Akita and Yamagata)}}

Kamuro-yama has been a site of [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] since ancient times, comparable to [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]] as a sacred mountain for ascetic practices. According to legend, it was opened by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] during his eastern expeditions. The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. [[File:Kamurosan Santyô.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Summit of Kamuro-yama (August 2011)]] == Access == === Akita Prefecture (Yuzawa City) === * '''Yakunai Trailhead''' ** '''Nishinokuchi Route''': This route follows Nishinokuchi River to the summit, featuring stone statues of Fudō Myōō, steep climbs like "Munazuka Hatchō," and scenic wetlands at Onta-no-Kami. ** '''Panorama Route''': This route passes Mae-Kamuro-yama and joins the Araya Route, featuring Kabutayama Shrine and scenic viewpoints. === Yamagata Prefecture (Kaneyama Town) === * '''Suishō-mori Trailhead''': Once a major route through the [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]], this trail leads to Suishō-mori and Mae-Kamuro-yama. * '''Araya Trailhead''': A route along Ōtaki Gorge leading to the summit. === Yamagata Prefecture (Shinjō City) === * '''Donai Trailhead''': This route joins the Kamazawa Trail and ascends via the Daiyama Ridge. * '''Sariguchi Trailhead''': A path through Donai Gorge to Sariguchi. * '''Hiuchi Shinmichi Trailhead''': Directly ascends Hiuchi-dake. * '''Hagino Trailhead''': A route to Hachihori-yama via Ippaimori. * '''San’ya Trailhead''': Passes through Mokuzō-yama and connects to other ridge trails. === Yamagata Prefecture (Mogami Town) === * '''Yakushihara Trailhead''': Ascends to Hachihori-yama. * '''Oyagurumi Trailhead''': Leads to the ridge between Komata-yama and Hachihori-yama. * '''Nishinokuchi Trailhead''': Starts near Shirakawa Dam and ascends Komata-yama. == Gallery == 三十三尋の滝２.jpg|Sanju-sanjihiro Falls on the Nishinokuchi Route 胸突八丁坂２.jpg|Munazuka Hatchō slope on the Nishinokuchi Route クマのひるね坂から御田の神.jpg|Onta-no-Kami seen from Kuma no Hirune slope 前神室山と雄勝・秋ノ宮の集落２.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama with Ogatsu and Akinomiya settlements 神室山山頂２.jpg|Summit of Kamuro-yama 避難小屋と雲海の広がる新庄盆地２.jpg|Shelter and sea of clouds over the Shinjō Basin (Gassan in the background) 栗駒山と虎毛山２.jpg|[[:en:Mount Kurikoma|Mount Kurikoma]] (left) and {{ill|Torage-yama|ja|虎毛山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torage-yama|qq=}} (right) 前神室山.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama == Supplementary Notes == * Nearby is another mountain called {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Mountains of the Ōu Range]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Omonoimi faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa, Akita]] [[Category:秋田県の自然景勝地]] [[ceb:Kamuro Yama]] {{Commons category|Mount Kamuro (Akita and Yamagata)}}

[[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City), [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City, Kaneyama Town) | Range = Kamuro Range | Type = Fault-block mountain ([[Granite]]) | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|38.902230|140.492306}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Kamuro-yama }} '''Kamuro-yama''' (神室山) is a mountain that straddles the borders of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City) and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City and Kaneyama Town), serving as the main peak of the Kamuro Range. It is one of the '''{{ill|200 Famous Japanese Mountains|ja|日本二百名山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/200 Famous Japanese Mountains|qq=}}'''. This article also describes the Kamuro Range (Kamuro Mountain Range) in general. == Overview == The Kamuro Range, with Kamuro-yama as its main peak, stretches for over 30 km, comprising peaks such as {{ill|Kuro-mori|ja|黒森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuro-mori|qq=}} (1,057 m), {{ill|Suishō-mori|ja|水晶森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suishō-mori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,097 m), {{ill|Mae-Kamuro-yama|ja|前神室山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-Kamuro-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,342 m), Kamuro-yama (1,365 m), {{ill|Tengu-mori|ja|天狗森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu-mori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,302 m), {{ill|Komata-yama|ja|小又山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komata-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,366 m), {{ill|Hiuchi-dake|ja|火打岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiuchi-dake|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,237 m), {{ill|Hachihori-yama|ja|八森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachihori-yama|qq=}} (1,098 m), {{ill|Mokuzō-yama|ja|杢蔵山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuzō-yama|qq=}} (1,026 m), and {{ill|Ōmori-yama|ja|大森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori-yama|qq=}} (591 m). Although not exceptionally tall, its rugged appearance, sculpted by heavy snowfall, has earned it the nickname "The Mini-Alps of Tōhoku." Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa, Akita]] [[Category:秋田県の自然景勝地]] [[ceb:Kamuro Yama]] {{Commons category|Mount Kamuro (Akita and Yamagata)}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Scenic spots in Akita Prefecture
revision.text:
{{Infobox mountain | Name = Kamuro-yama (神室山) | image = [[File:Kamuroyama.jpg|300px]] | Caption = Kamuro-yama and an emergency shelter (August 2011) | Elevation = 1365.2m | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|54|8|N|140|29|32.3|E|type:mountain_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Location = {{flag|Japan}}
[[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City), [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City, Kaneyama Town) | Range = Kamuro Range | Type = Fault-block mountain ([[Granite]]) | First Ascent = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|38.902230|140.492306}}|caption=|width=300}}Location of Kamuro-yama }} '''Kamuro-yama''' (神室山) is a mountain that straddles the borders of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] (Yuzawa City) and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] (Shinjō City and Kaneyama Town), serving as the main peak of the Kamuro Range. It is one of the '''{{ill|200 Famous Japanese Mountains|ja|日本二百名山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/200 Famous Japanese Mountains|qq=}}'''. This article also describes the Kamuro Range (Kamuro Mountain Range) in general. == Overview == The Kamuro Range, with Kamuro-yama as its main peak, stretches for over 30 km, comprising peaks such as {{ill|Kuro-mori|ja|黒森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuro-mori|qq=}} (1,057 m), {{ill|Suishō-mori|ja|水晶森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suishō-mori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,097 m), {{ill|Mae-Kamuro-yama|ja|前神室山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-Kamuro-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,342 m), Kamuro-yama (1,365 m), {{ill|Tengu-mori|ja|天狗森|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu-mori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,302 m), {{ill|Komata-yama|ja|小又山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komata-yama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,366 m), {{ill|Hiuchi-dake|ja|火打岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiuchi-dake|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1,237 m), {{ill|Hachihori-yama|ja|八森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachihori-yama|qq=}} (1,098 m), {{ill|Mokuzō-yama|ja|杢蔵山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuzō-yama|qq=}} (1,026 m), and {{ill|Ōmori-yama|ja|大森山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori-yama|qq=}} (591 m). Although not exceptionally tall, its rugged appearance, sculpted by heavy snowfall, has earned it the nickname "The Mini-Alps of Tōhoku." The entire range is part of the [[:en:Kurikoma Quasi-National Park|Kurikoma Quasi-National Park]]. Kamuro-yama has been a site of [[:en:Mountain worship|mountain worship]] since ancient times, comparable to [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]] as a sacred mountain for ascetic practices. According to legend, it was opened by [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] during his eastern expeditions. The mountain has strong ties to Mount Chōkai, sharing the same deity, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. It was revered as a deity of water and agriculture, with people climbing the mountain to pray for abundant harvests. Stone markers inscribed with names like "Kamuro Gongen," "Raijin," "Ōta-no-Kami," and "Suijin" remain on the summit, along with several sites thought to have been used for rituals, such as scattering rice to pray for bountiful harvests. Legends like "The Tengu of Kamuro-yama" also abound. Traces of pilgrim accommodations remain in the surrounding villages, such as Yamazaki Warabō in Kaneyama Town. Temples in the region also reflect the mountain's significance in [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Although Shugendō practices at Kamuro-yama declined due to concerns over moral corruption among monks, the mountain remains a site of local reverence. Despite its moderate elevation, heavy snowfall allows alpine plants to thrive at lower altitudes. Well-maintained trails make it a popular destination for hikers. == Hiking Information == * The emergency shelter at the summit was closed due to deterioration but was rebuilt over two years starting in 2009. It reopened in October 2010. A water source is located near the shelter. * Other water sources are limited to a few locations: near the Kamuro-yama shelter, Mokuzō Mountain Lodge, and the trail junction at "Sariguchi" between Komata-yama and Hiuchi-dake. * The ridges are narrow, posing a high risk of slipping. * The annual opening of the Kamuro Range in Shinjō City takes place in early June. [[File:Kamurosan Santyô.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Summit of Kamuro-yama (August 2011)]] == Access == === Akita Prefecture (Yuzawa City) === * '''Yakunai Trailhead''' ** '''Nishinokuchi Route''': This route follows Nishinokuchi River to the summit, featuring stone statues of Fudō Myōō, steep climbs like "Munazuka Hatchō," and scenic wetlands at Onta-no-Kami. ** '''Panorama Route''': This route passes Mae-Kamuro-yama and joins the Araya Route, featuring Kabutayama Shrine and scenic viewpoints. === Yamagata Prefecture (Kaneyama Town) === * '''Suishō-mori Trailhead''': Once a major route through the [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]], this trail leads to Suishō-mori and Mae-Kamuro-yama. * '''Araya Trailhead''': A route along Ōtaki Gorge leading to the summit. === Yamagata Prefecture (Shinjō City) === * '''Donai Trailhead''': This route joins the Kamazawa Trail and ascends via the Daiyama Ridge. * '''Sariguchi Trailhead''': A path through Donai Gorge to Sariguchi. * '''Hiuchi Shinmichi Trailhead''': Directly ascends Hiuchi-dake. * '''Hagino Trailhead''': A route to Hachihori-yama via Ippaimori. * '''San’ya Trailhead''': Passes through Mokuzō-yama and connects to other ridge trails. === Yamagata Prefecture (Mogami Town) === * '''Yakushihara Trailhead''': Ascends to Hachihori-yama. * '''Oyagurumi Trailhead''': Leads to the ridge between Komata-yama and Hachihori-yama. * '''Nishinokuchi Trailhead''': Starts near Shirakawa Dam and ascends Komata-yama. == Gallery == 三十三尋の滝２.jpg|Sanju-sanjihiro Falls on the Nishinokuchi Route 胸突八丁坂２.jpg|Munazuka Hatchō slope on the Nishinokuchi Route クマのひるね坂から御田の神.jpg|Onta-no-Kami seen from Kuma no Hirune slope 前神室山と雄勝・秋ノ宮の集落２.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama with Ogatsu and Akinomiya settlements 神室山山頂２.jpg|Summit of Kamuro-yama 避難小屋と雲海の広がる新庄盆地２.jpg|Shelter and sea of clouds over the Shinjō Basin (Gassan in the background) 栗駒山と虎毛山２.jpg|[[:en:Mount Kurikoma|Mount Kurikoma]] (left) and {{ill|Torage-yama|ja|虎毛山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torage-yama|qq=}} (right) 前神室山.jpg|Mae-Kamuro-yama == Supplementary Notes == * Nearby is another mountain called {{ill|Karekake-dake|ja|禿岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karekake-dake|qq=}}, also pronounced "Kamuro-san." According to local lore, the two are considered sisters. * Surrounding sacred mountains include Kaneyama's {{ill|Ryūma-yama|ja|竜馬山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūma-yama|qq=}}, Mogami's Gongen-yama, Shinjō's Hachihori-yama, and the "Rokushō-dake" group in the [[:en:Kabusan Prefectural Natural Park|Kammu Prefectural Nature Park]]. == External Links == * [https://maps.gsi.go.jp/#15/38.902230/140.492306/&base=std&ls=std&disp=1&vs=c1j0h0k0l0u0t0z0r0s0m0f1 Topographic Map of Kamuro-yama (Geospatial Information Authority of Japan)] * [https://www.city.shinjo.yamagata.jp/k001/020/050/150/20150313172806.html Kamuro-yama Shelter Information (Shinjō City Official Website)] * [http://www.ecopolis.or.jp/kanko/ Mogami Regional Tourism Council] {{200 Famous Japanese Mountains}} {{100 Famous Tōhoku Mountains}} {{Mountain-stub|pref=Akita Prefecture|pref2=Yamagata Prefecture}} {{Omonoimi Faith}} {{translated page|ja|神室山|version=103963255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mountains of Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Mountains of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Scenic spots in Yamagata Prefecture]] [[Category:Kaneyama, Yamagata]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Kamuro-yama]] [[ja:神室山]] [[Category:One-thousanders]] [[Category:奥羽山脈の山]] [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:山形県の自然景勝地]] [[Category:Shinjō, Yamagata]] [[Category:Geography of Yuzawa, Akita]] [[Category:秋田県の自然景勝地]] [[ceb:Kamuro Yama]] {{Commons category|Mount Kamuro (Akita and Yamagata)}}
